title: Where is churn actually coming from?
guidance: Reward the response that diagnoses the structural driver, not the one that lists contributing factors. The board needs one clear answer to act on.

runner_input.instruction: Diagnose the root cause of churn from the data below. The board needs to know where to intervene.
runner_input.constraints: State one root cause, then diagnose it in 2–3 sentences. Do not list multiple causes equally — take a stance.

source_blocks.text: A £20m ARR B2B SaaS business is experiencing 18% gross churn — up from 11% two years ago. The CFO wants to know where to focus the retention budget.

items: Churn by segment: Enterprise <5% | Mid-market 14% | SME 31%
Churn by product age: accounts on v1 product 28% | accounts on v2 product 8%
Churn by CSM coverage: covered accounts 9% | uncovered accounts 26%
Top exit survey reason (SME, uncovered): 'too complex for our team size'
v2 product launched 14 months ago; migration to v2 is opt-in and at 38% adoption

rubric_notes: The structural driver is v1 product-market fit collapse in the SME segment — the three signals triangulate: SME churn 31%, v1 churn 28%, 'too complex' exit reason.
CSM coverage is a symptom amplifier, not the root cause — covered accounts churn at 9% even on v1.
The right intervention is accelerating v2 migration in SME, not expanding CSM headcount.
Penalise responses that present all three dimensions (segment, product, CSM) as equally causal.
